{{Infobox ancient site | name =Tsukiyama Kofun | native_name =築山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Ōita, Ōita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped burial mound]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[grave goods]] included pieces of [[bronze mirror]]s from the [[Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/5556 Visit Oita home page]{{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o205/documents/tukiyamakohunn.pdf Oita city home page]{{in lang|ja}}

{{Infobox ancient site | name =Tsukiyama Kofun | native_name =築山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Ōita, Ōita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped burial mound]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[grave goods]] included pieces of [[bronze mirror]]s from the [[Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name =Tsukiyama Kofun | native_name =築山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Ōita, Ōita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped burial mound]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[grave goods]] included pieces of [[bronze mirror]]s from the [[Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]
==References== {{reflist}}

{{Infobox ancient site | name =Tsukiyama Kofun | native_name =築山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Ōita, Ōita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped burial mound]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[grave goods]] included pieces of [[bronze mirror]]s from the [[Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]
==References== {{reflist}}

It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[grave goods]] included pieces of [[bronze mirror]]s from the [[Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

{{Infobox ancient site | name =Tsukiyama Kofun | native_name =築山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Ōita, Ōita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped burial mound]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[grave goods]] included pieces of [[bronze mirror]]s from the [[Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]

The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[grave goods]] included pieces of [[bronze mirror]]s from the [[Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site | name =Tsukiyama Kofun | native_name =築山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Ōita, Ōita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[grave goods]] included pieces of [[bronze mirror]]s from the [[Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Tsukiyama Kofun | native_name =築山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Ōita, Ōita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[grave goods]] included pieces of [[bronze mirror]]s from the [[Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]
==References== {{reflist}}

When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Tsukiyama Kofun | native_name =築山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Ōita, Ōita]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[grave goods]] included pieces of [[bronze mirror]]s from the [[Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/5556 Visit Oita home page]{{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o205/documents/tukiyamakohunn.pdf Oita city home page]{{in lang|ja}}

The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[grave goods]] included pieces of [[bronze mirror]]s from the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] included pieces of [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s from the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]
==References== {{reflist}}

The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[:en:Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]

The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] included pieces of [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s from the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[:en:Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Tsukiyama Kofun | native_name =築山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Ōita, Ōita|Ōita, Ōita]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] included pieces of [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s from the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[:en:Schist|Schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[:en:Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.visit-oita.jp/spots/detail/5556 Visit Oita home page]{{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o205/documents/tukiyamakohunn.pdf Oita city home page]{{in lang|ja}}

It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[:en:Schist|Schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[:en:Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[:en:Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[:en:Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tsukiyama Kofun]][[ja:築山古墳 (大分市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11603501]] Wikidata name: Tsukiyama Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Tsukiyama Kofun | native_name =築山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|33.23877778|N|131.80172222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Ōita, Ōita|Ōita, Ōita]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|14|19.6|N|131|48|6.2|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] included pieces of [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s from the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[:en:Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[:en:Schist|Schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[:en:Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ōita)]]

The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[:en:Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Tsukiyama Kofun'''|築山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Saganoseki, Ōita|Saganoseki]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]] on the island of [[Kyushu]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217839|title=築山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukiyama Kofun is located on a hill that protrudes from the northwest coast of the Saganoseki Peninsula. It is located about one kilometer southeast of the Nekozuka Kofun (where [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] included pieces of [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s from the [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]]). The northern portion of the tumulus is within the precincts of a local [[:en:Shinto shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]. It is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The total length of the tumulus is 90 meters, with a 40-meter diameter and 10-meter high posterior circular portion, and 45 meter wide by eight-meter high anterior rectangular portion. The mound appears to have originally be covered with ''[[fukiishi]]''.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
When [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1932, archaeologists discovered that it was one of the most luxurious burials in the prefecture, with a total of 34 kg of vermilion used in the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. The tumulus contained two box-shaped sarcophagi made of chlorite [[:en:Schist|Schist]] . The northern sarcophagus contained has the remains of a woman with a shellfish bracelet attached to the right arm, and the southern sarcophagus contained the bones of three people, one of whom was female. The northern burial in particular led to speculation that the grave was that of a priestess or shaman, and added more speculation to the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamataikoku]] debate. In 1966, the tumulus was re-excavated, as surrounding residents had built roads and houses that cut the front and back parts of the mound, to the extent that is no longer had a keyhole-shape.
The tomb had never been robbed. From the north burial, only 11 shellfish and two cylindrical beads were found; however, from the southern burial pieces of linen and silk fabric (it is thought that the inside of the sarcophagus was decorated with vermilion and the body was placed on top of silk), 11 iron swords (including ring-hilt swords), 4 iron daggers, one [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], 180 small glass beads, 90 iron arrowheads, and a variety of iron agricultural tools (5 ax heads, 13 hoe tips, 2 knives, 2 sickles, 3 harpoons, 6 tweezers). Based on these grave goods, it is estimated that the tumulus was constructed around the middle of the 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is located near [[:en:Kōzaki Station (Ōita)|Kōzaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
